blocks.statement: I can compare an asteroid's KE to the energy required to vaporize its mass and explain why surface-area-to-mass ratio sets the size threshold for atmospheric burn-up.

blocks.whatWeKnow: 2026-XJ is HUGE (m ≈ 10⁹ kg, r ≈ 50 m). The Chelyabinsk meteor was tiny (m ≈ 10⁴ kg) — it burst in the atmosphere with no crater.
blocks.connection: Today's question: at 2026-XJ's size, does the atmosphere matter at all? Where's the size threshold between 'burns up' and 'reaches ground'?

blocks.markdown:
## Why pebbles burn up and boulders don't

A rock entering the atmosphere at 15 km/s carries an enormous KE = ½mv². Atmospheric drag does NEGATIVE work on it, and every joule the drag removes shows up as **heat at the rock's surface**.

Here's the surprise: per kilogram, EVERY rock at 15 km/s carries the same KE — about 1.1 × 10⁸ J/kg — which is roughly **13× the energy needed to vaporize that kilogram** (E_vap ≈ 8.6 × 10⁶ J/kg, using c_rock·ΔT + L_v). So why doesn't everything vaporize?

**Geometry.** Drag heating happens at the SURFACE; mass lives in the VOLUME. For a sphere, A/m = 3/(ρ·r):

- A **1-kg pebble** is essentially ALL surface — high A/m, huge drag per kg, every gram gets cooked. It vaporizes completely. (That's a shooting star.)
- A **10⁹-kg asteroid** has 1000× less surface area per kg. The heat chews on the outside (**ablation**) while the core never feels it. It loses < 1% of its mass and arrives at the ground at nearly full KE.

The per-kg energy ratio is identical for both. The size threshold isn't about energy — it's about WHERE the heat can reach.

terms.definition: A friction-like force that air exerts on a fast-moving object. Does NEGATIVE work — takes KE away. The KE removed becomes HEAT at the object's surface. A satellite re-entering at 7.8 km/s heats its surface to ~2000 °C from drag — this is why re-entry shielding matters. The faster the object, the MORE drag per second (drag ∝ v² roughly).
terms.cognate: Sp. arrastre atmosférico · Pt. arrasto atmosférico

terms.definition: For a sphere, A/m = 3/(ρ·r). SMALL rocks have HIGH A/m → lots of drag per kg → vaporize. BIG rocks have LOW A/m → little drag per kg → survive. A 1-kg pebble has ~1000× more A/m than a 10⁹-kg asteroid: the pebble burns away entirely; the asteroid loses < 1% of its mass to ablation.

The process by which a rock's outer layer vaporizes during atmospheric entry, carrying heat AWAY from the rock as molten/vapor mass. The Apollo capsules used ablative heat shields — designed to char and vaporize, carrying heat away rather than letting it into the spacecraft. Ablation HELPS: without it, big asteroids would heat all the way through.

blocks.title: It's drag PER KG, not total drag
blocks.markdown: It's not the rock's TOTAL drag force that decides survival — it's the drag force PER KG of rock. Big rocks have lots of drag too, but they have even more mass.

links.label: Kinetic energy
links.sublabel: ½mv² of the incoming rock at ~15 km/s

links.label: Drag does negative work
links.sublabel: air resistance strips KE during entry

links.label: Ablation / vaporization
links.sublabel: outer layer vaporizes, carrying heat away; high A/m → ALL of it goes

blocks.instruction: Draw three rocks entering the atmosphere: a 1-kg pebble, a 1000-kg boulder, and 10⁹-kg 2026-XJ. Compare them.
prompts: Why does the 1-kg pebble vaporize completely?
Why does the 10⁹-kg asteroid arrive at essentially full mass?
Mark the size threshold between 'burns up' and 'reaches ground' on your diagram

blocks.prompt: THE BIG COMPARISON — KE VS. VAPORIZATION ENERGY. Three rocks at v = 15 km/s. For each, compute (1) KE = ½mv² and (2) energy to vaporize: E_vap = m · (c_rock · ΔT + L_v_rock) where c_rock ≈ 800 J/kg·°C, ΔT ≈ 2000 °C, L_v ≈ 7 × 10⁶ J/kg. Compare ratios.
blocks.equation: KE = ½ m v²   AND   E_vap = m · 8.6 × 10⁶ J/kg
blocks.work:
ROCK A: m = 1 kg
  KE = 0.5 · 1 · (1.5e4)² = 1.125 × 10⁸ J
  E_vap = 1 · 8.6e6 = 8.6 × 10⁶ J
  ratio KE / E_vap ≈ 13 → KE is 13× more than needed to vaporize. BURNS UP.

ROCK B: m = 1000 kg
  KE = 0.5 · 1000 · (1.5e4)² = 1.125 × 10¹¹ J
  E_vap = 1000 · 8.6e6 = 8.6 × 10⁹ J
  ratio = 13 → same ratio per kg — but most heat goes into the surface, not the core. Only OUTER ~10% vaporizes.

ROCK C: m = 10⁹ kg (2026-XJ)
  KE = 0.5 · 1e9 · (1.5e4)² ≈ 1.125 × 10¹⁷ J
  E_vap = 1e9 · 8.6e6 = 8.6 × 10¹⁵ J
  ratio = 13 → surface ablation is < 1% of mass. REACHES GROUND.
blocks.answer: Small rocks: ALL surface, vaporize entirely. Big rocks: surface ablates, core survives. The ratio per kg is the SAME — it's geometry (A/m) that decides.

blocks.title: Chelyabinsk, February 2013
blocks.markdown: The Chelyabinsk meteor was ~10⁴ kg — small enough that it AIRBURST 30 km up. No crater. Just a shockwave that broke 100,000 windows and injured 1500 people. Big rocks behave differently — Day 11 explains how.

blocks.prompt: WOULD A 10-KG ROCK SURVIVE? A 10-kg rock at v = 15 km/s. Compute KE, E_vap, and the ratio. Will it reach the ground intact? Why?
blocks.givenHint: m = 10 kg · v = 1.5 × 10⁴ m/s · E_vap per kg ≈ 8.6 × 10⁶ J/kg
blocks.equationHint: KE = ½mv² and E_vap = m · 8.6 × 10⁶ J/kg. Compare the ratio — then think about A/m: is a 10-kg rock closer to the pebble or the asteroid?

blocks.prompt: Why does a 1-kg rock burn up but a 10⁹-kg asteroid reach the ground? Reason in terms of surface-area-to-mass ratio.
blocks.frame: A small rock burns up because its surface area per kg is ___, so drag per kg is ___ and ALL its KE becomes heat at the surface. 2026-XJ's surface area per kg is ___, so only ___ % ablates and the rest reaches the ground.
